name: Robotics & Industrial Automation

asset_categories.description: Motors, servos, grippers, and end effectors that produce physical motion — safety-critical, direct physical harm potential
Sensors providing environmental awareness — cameras, lidar, force-torque sensors, proximity sensors, encoders
Path planning and trajectory generation algorithms — translates AI decisions into physical movements
Safety-rated PLC/controller enforcing safety functions (emergency stop, speed limiting, force limiting, safety zones)
Teach pendants, HMIs, voice commands, gesture recognition — operator interaction points
Virtual replica used for simulation, testing, and predictive maintenance — may diverge from physical twin
Industrial network connecting robot controllers, PLCs, SCADA — EtherNet/IP, PROFINET, Modbus, OPC UA

threat_sources.guidance: Search for IEC 62443 controls applicable to industrial automation and control systems (IACS). Use get_energy_standard for zone and conduit security requirements. IEC 62443 security levels (SL-1 through SL-4) map to attack sophistication — align with feasibility assessment.

impact_calibration.safety: Robots with actuators that can reach humans are inherently safety-critical. Use ISO 12100 risk estimation: severity of harm (S1 reversible, S2 irreversible) x frequency of exposure (F1 rare, F2 frequent) x possibility of avoidance (P1 possible, P2 not possible). Collaborative robots (cobots) in shared workspaces: safety >= 3 for any threat affecting motion control. Industrial robots behind safety fencing: safety depends on whether safety PLC can be bypassed. Any threat that could disable safety functions (e-stop, speed limiting, force limiting) = safety 5.
impact_calibration.operational: Robot downtime in manufacturing directly impacts production throughput. Calculate: hourly production value x expected downtime. Automotive manufacturing lines: ~EUR 20K/hour per cell. Warehouse automation: throughput degradation cascades to SLA breaches.
impact_calibration.financial: Include production loss, equipment damage (robots are EUR 50K-500K each), rework costs, and worker compensation claims. EU Machinery Regulation 2023/1230 non-compliance blocks CE marking — cannot sell or operate in EU.
impact_calibration.regulatory: EU Machinery Regulation 2023/1230 (replaces Directive 2006/42/EC from 2027-01-20) — mandatory EHSR compliance including cybersecurity requirements in new Article 1.1.9. IEC 62443 increasingly referenced by regulators for industrial cybersecurity. ISO 13849 for safety-related parts of control systems. Any threat compromising safety functions may require machine shutdown until resolved.

feasibility_calibration: Industrial control networks are often segmented but not air-gapped — OPC UA, MQTT, and cloud dashboards create attack surface. Adjust window_of_opportunity based on network architecture: air-gapped (difficult, 3), segmented with firewall (moderate, 2), flat network with IT/OT convergence (easy, 1). Physical access attacks on teach pendants and USB ports rate high feasibility in shared facilities. ROS (Robot Operating System) has minimal built-in authentication — ROS 2 DDS security is optional and often disabled. Supply chain attacks on ROS packages and controller firmware are a growing concern. Reinforcement learning control policies are vulnerable to adversarial perturbation of sensor inputs — published research exists for robotic manipulation tasks.

compliance_frameworks.name: IEC 62443 (Industrial Automation and Control Systems Security)
key_requirements: 3-2 — Security risk assessment and system design
3-3 — System security requirements and security levels (SL-1 to SL-4)
4-2 — Technical security requirements for IACS components

compliance_frameworks.name: EU Machinery Regulation 2023/1230
key_requirements: Annex III 1.1.9 — Protection against corruption (cybersecurity EHSR)
Annex III 1.2.1 — Safety and reliability of control systems
Annex III 1.3.7 — Risks from moving parts

compliance_frameworks.name: ISO/TS 15066:2016
key_requirements: 5.5.4 — Biomechanical limits (contact forces per body region)
Annex A — Body model and pain onset thresholds

report_addenda.title: Safety Function Threat Mapping
report_addenda.guidance: Identify all safety functions (e-stop, speed limiting, force limiting, safety zones, safety-rated monitored stop) defined in ISO 10218 / ISO/TS 15066. For each R3-R5 threat, assess whether it could compromise a safety function. Map to ISO 13849 performance levels — a compromised safety function may need PL upgrade or architectural redesign.

report_addenda.title: IEC 62443 Security Level Mapping
report_addenda.guidance: Map identified threats to IEC 62443 foundational requirements (FR 1-7: identification, use control, system integrity, data confidentiality, data flow, event response, resource availability). Determine target security level (SL-T) based on threat feasibility: very_high feasibility → minimum SL-3, high → SL-2, medium → SL-2, low → SL-1. Show the gap between current security level (SL-C) and target.

report_addenda.title: EU Machinery Regulation EHSR Compliance
report_addenda.guidance: Map cybersecurity threats and controls to the Essential Health and Safety Requirements (EHSRs) of EU Machinery Regulation 2023/1230, focusing on Annex III 1.1.9 (protection against corruption). Show traceability from TARA risk treatment to EHSR compliance. Note: 2023/1230 becomes mandatory 2027-01-20, replacing Directive 2006/42/EC.
